statusLine: EU fuel security remains under strain with 19 of 27 countries holding critical reserves in at least one fuel type, despite slight improvements in some sectors.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security continues to face significant pressure as oil prices have surged 23.4% since late February to $89.46/barrel, with 19 of 27 EU countries maintaining critical stock levels in at least one fuel category. While EU-wide averages for petrol (96.3 days) and diesel (88.8 days) hover near the mandatory 90-day minimum, jet fuel remains particularly tight at 87.3 days across the bloc.

The situation varies dramatically across member states. Luxembourg, Poland, and Romania face the most severe shortages, with all three fuel types at critical levels. Luxembourg's reserves are particularly concerning, with petrol at just 38 days and jet fuel at 43.1 days. Conversely, several countries maintain robust reserves: Estonia holds 320 days of jet fuel, Slovenia has 365 days, and Latvia maintains 250.6 days of petrol reserves.

Fuel prices reflect the underlying supply tensions, with EU average petrol at €1.786/L and diesel at €1.804/L. The price spread across the bloc is substantial, ranging from Malta's €1.34/L petrol to Denmark's €2.392/L, indicating varying national taxation policies and supply chain pressures. The recent strategic reserve releases by several countries following Middle East supply disruptions appear to have provided temporary relief but have not resolved the underlying inventory challenges.
keyPoints: 19 of 27 EU countries maintain critical fuel stock levels in at least one category, with Luxembourg, Poland, and Romania facing shortages across all three fuel types
Jet fuel remains the most constrained fuel EU-wide at 87.3 days, below the 90-day mandatory minimum
Oil prices have surged 23.4% since late February to $89.46/barrel, reflecting ongoing Middle East supply disruption impacts
Significant variation exists between countries: Estonia holds 320 days of jet fuel while Poland has just 22.5 days
Fuel prices show wide disparities across the bloc, from Malta's €1.34/L petrol to Denmark's €2.392/L
